name: Kadru
shortDescription: Kadru is the mother of the Nagas (serpents) in Hindu mythology, known for her role in the story of the churning of the ocean.
description: Kadru, in Hindu mythology, is a prominent figure primarily known as the mother of the Nagas, the serpent race. She is the daughter of Daksha and one of the thirteen wives of the sage Kashyapa. Her story is primarily narrated in the Mahabharata and various Puranas. The most significant myth associated with Kadru involves a bet with her sister Vinata, the mother of Garuda. This bet concerned the color of the horse Uchchaihshravas, which emerged during the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana). Kadru deceitfully used her Naga sons to darken the horse's tail, thus winning the bet and enslaving Vinata. This act of deception and the subsequent enslavement of Vinata forms a crucial part of Hindu mythology, highlighting themes of cunning, power, and the complex relationships between deities and their offspring. Kadru's role as the mother of the Nagas firmly establishes her connection to the serpent world, a realm often associated with both danger and wisdom.
subtitle: Serpent Mother, Deceptive Queen
realm: Patala (the netherworld, home of the Nagas)
role: Mother of the Nagas, instigator of conflict

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Kadru's origin is traced back to Daksha, a Prajapati, and she became one of the wives of Kashyapa, a revered sage. Her primary myth revolves around the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana). When the divine horse Uchchaihshravas emerged, Kadru and her sister Vinata argued over its color. Kadru claimed the horse's tail was black, while Vinata insisted it was white. They wagered their freedom on the outcome. Kadru, knowing the tail was indeed white, commanded her Naga sons to cling to the horse's tail, making it appear black from a distance. This deception led to Vinata losing the bet and becoming Kadru's slave. This story highlights Kadru's manipulative nature and the power she wielded through her numerous offspring.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Kadru's role is significant in establishing the lineage and characteristics of the Naga race. As their mother, she is responsible for their existence and their inherent nature, which often includes both wisdom and a propensity for causing harm. The story of her deceit and the enslavement of Vinata serves as a cautionary tale, illustrating the consequences of dishonesty and the abuse of power. Her story is also important in the context of the larger narrative of the Samudra Manthana, as it sets the stage for the conflict between the Nagas and Garuda, a recurring theme in Hindu mythology. This conflict represents the eternal struggle between good and evil, knowledge and ignorance.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Kadru symbolizes the power of motherhood, particularly in its darker aspects. Her manipulation of her children to achieve her own ends reflects a distorted version of maternal protectiveness. The Nagas themselves are symbolic of primal forces, both creative and destructive, and Kadru, as their mother, embodies this duality. The bet with Vinata and its outcome can be interpreted as a representation of the conflict between truth and falsehood, and the deceptive nature of appearances. The story also touches upon themes of karma and consequence, as Kadru's actions ultimately lead to the ongoing animosity between her descendants and those of Vinata. The serpent race often represents the earthly and the material, contrasting with the bird race of Vinata who represent the heavenly and spiritual.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Kadru's legacy continues to resonate in Hindu art, literature, and religious practices. The Nagas, as her offspring, are frequently depicted in temples and stories, often guarding sacred spaces or representing the kundalini energy. The tale of Kadru and Vinata remains a popular narrative, illustrating moral lessons about honesty, integrity, and the consequences of deceit. In modern interpretations, Kadru can be seen as a complex character, representing the challenges and responsibilities of motherhood, as well as the potential for both good and evil within familial relationships. The story of the churning of the ocean, in which Kadru plays a pivotal role, is still enacted and celebrated in various Hindu festivals and rituals.

sources.author: Vyasadeva
sources.description: The primary source for Kadru's story, detailing her lineage, the bet with Vinata, and the birth of the Nagas.

sources.author: Various Authors
sources.description: The Puranas provide further details and variations of the Kadru and Vinata narrative, expanding on the origins and characteristics of the Nagas.

associations.name: Serpents (Nagas)
associations.significance: Kadru is the mother of all Nagas, establishing a direct and fundamental connection.

associations.name: Deception
associations.significance: Kadru's deceitful actions in the bet with Vinata are a defining characteristic.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Echidna
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Mother of monsters, similar to Kadru's role as the mother of the Nagas.

companions.name: Kashyapa
companions.description: Sage Kashyapa is Kadru's husband and the father of her Naga offspring.

companions.name: Vinata
companions.description: Vinata is Kadru's sister and rival, whose enslavement is central to Kadru's story.

cultural.modernLegacy: Kadru's story continues to be retold in popular culture through books, movies, and television shows, often highlighting the themes of deception, power, and the complex relationships between mothers and their children. The Nagas, as her offspring, remain a significant symbol in Hindu art and religion.
